chunk_summary: This section discusses the differentiation and management of breastfeeding-associated neonatal jaundice, including breastfeeding jaundice and breast milk jaundice, with emphasis on evaluation, treatment thresholds, and breastfeeding continuation strategies.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical differentiation between breastfeeding jaundice and breast milk jaundice.
Learn management strategies to optimize milk intake and prevent severe hyperbilirubinemia.
Recognize when to escalate evaluation for underlying causes such as hemolysis or cholestasis.
Apply phototherapy guidelines and bilirubin nomograms in neonatal jaundice management.
key_facts: Breastfeeding jaundice typically peaks around days 3–5 and is associated with suboptimal milk intake and delayed meconium clearance.
Management includes feeding 8–12 times per day, correcting latch, and using breast compressions; supplementation may be needed if intake remains inadequate.
Phototherapy should not be delayed; assess bilirubin using TcB or serum TSB and apply age-specific nomograms.
Breast milk jaundice starts after the first week and can persist for weeks in thriving infants; breastfeeding should continue without routine interruption.
Prolonged jaundice beyond 14 days in term infants (or 21 days in preterm infants) requires direct bilirubin measurement to exclude cholestasis.
reference_quote: Jaundice is common in breastfed infants, and breastfeeding should usually continue during evaluation and treatment. (ABM Clinical Protocol #22)
document_key_topics: Breast milk jaundice and prolonged jaundice evaluation
Exclusion of underlying causes like hemolysis and cholestasis
